rewrites.before:
# Caching strategies for read-heavy services
<!-- stay:pITD9gg5 hash=sha256:434dcf91de4b -->

A cache sits between a client and a slower source of truth. When a service reads the
same data far more often than it writes, a cache turns most reads into cheap in-memory
lookups instead of round trips to a database or an upstream API. The art is not adding
a cache, it is deciding when an entry is allowed to go stale and how the system notices.
<!-- stay:GGLcjD5y hash=sha256:88669a8b6809 -->

## Cache-aside
<!-- stay:OZoAAY5s hash=sha256:fcbb831cf126 -->

The most common pattern is cache-aside, sometimes called lazy loading. The application
checks the cache first. On a hit it returns the cached value. On a miss it reads the
source of truth, stores the result in the cache, and returns it. The cache only ever
holds data that has actually been requested, so memory tracks the working set rather
than the whole dataset.
<!-- stay:VGiayjA3 hash=sha256:ecc2609e25b5 -->

The weakness of cache-aside is the window between a write to the source and the next
read. If a record changes in the database but the cached copy is not evicted, readers
keep seeing the old value until the entry expires. Teams usually pair cache-aside with
an explicit invalidation step on write, or a short time-to-live that bounds how stale
a value can get.
<!-- stay:b4y0juaJ hash=sha256:1242651a0d6e -->

## Write-through and write-behind
<!-- stay:pdcx3cIW hash=sha256:aea64a12885d -->

A write-through cache accepts the write itself, persists it to the source of truth, and
updates its own copy in the same operation. Reads after a write are always consistent
because the cache was part of the write path. The cost is write latency: every write
now pays for both the cache and the backing store.
<!-- stay:56caDnqS hash=sha256:c7c36eff7dc0 -->

Write-behind relaxes that by acknowledging the write immediately and flushing to the
source asynchronously. Throughput improves and write latency drops, but a crash before
the flush loses data, so write-behind suits metrics and counters more than orders and
payments.
<!-- stay:DlawgOHC hash=sha256:f5ddc23e0a5c -->

## Invalidation
<!-- stay:LNd9a7gu hash=sha256:5483bf1b3a15 -->

Phil Karlton's quip that there are only two hard problems in computer science, cache
invalidation and naming things, has survived because it is true. The safest default is
a time-to-live short enough that stale reads are tolerable and long enough that the hit
rate stays high. Event-driven invalidation, where a write publishes a message that
evicts the affected keys, is more precise but adds a moving part that can fail silently
and leave the cache serving stale data with no expiry to save it.
<!-- stay:EBbxHXpf hash=sha256:703d21f69911 -->

Whatever the scheme, measure the hit rate in production. A cache with a low hit rate is
pure overhead: it pays the cost of an extra lookup on every read and rarely avoids the
expensive path it was meant to protect.
<!-- stay:scEyHzyH hash=sha256:cc8b69af499a -->

rewrites.after:
# Caching strategies for read-heavy services
<!-- stay:pITD9gg5 hash=sha256:434dcf91de4b -->

A cache lives between a client and a slower authoritative data store. When reads vastly outnumber writes, a cache converts the majority of those reads into fast in-memory operations rather than expensive round trips to a database or external API. The real challenge is not introducing a cache in the first place — it is determining how long entries may remain stale and how the system detects that staleness.
<!-- stay:GGLcjD5y hash=sha256:88669a8b6809 -->

## Cache-aside
<!-- stay:OZoAAY5s hash=sha256:fcbb831cf126 -->

Lazy loading, also known as cache-aside, is the pattern teams reach for most often. The application first consults the cache; if the entry is present, that value is returned immediately. If nothing is found, the application fetches the data from the authoritative source, writes it into the cache, and hands it back to the caller. Because entries are only populated on demand, the cache naturally mirrors the working set rather than duplicating the entire dataset.
<!-- stay:VGiayjA3 hash=sha256:ecc2609e25b5 -->

The fundamental problem with cache-aside is the gap that opens between a write to the source of truth and the next read. When a record is updated in the database but its cached counterpart is not removed, every subsequent reader receives the outdated value until that entry finally expires. To contain this, teams typically combine cache-aside with an explicit eviction step triggered at write time, or they set a tight time-to-live that caps the maximum age of any cached value.
<!-- stay:b4y0juaJ hash=sha256:1242651a0d6e -->

## Write-through and write-behind
<!-- stay:pdcx3cIW hash=sha256:aea64a12885d -->

In a write-through cache, incoming writes are handled by the cache itself, which simultaneously persists the data to the backing store and refreshes its own copy. Because the cache participates directly in the write path, any subsequent read is guaranteed to be consistent. The trade-off is added write latency, since every write must complete against both the cache and the underlying storage before it is acknowledged.
<!-- stay:56caDnqS hash=sha256:c7c36eff7dc0 -->

Write-behind trades that durability guarantee for performance by acknowledging writes instantly and deferring the flush to the backing store to a background process. This reduces write latency and raises throughput, but any data not yet flushed is lost if the process crashes. That risk makes write-behind a natural fit for things like metrics and counters, and a poor choice for financial transactions or order records.
<!-- stay:DlawgOHC hash=sha256:f5ddc23e0a5c -->

## Invalidation
<!-- stay:LNd9a7gu hash=sha256:5483bf1b3a15 -->

Phil Karlton's observation that cache invalidation and naming things are the only two genuinely hard problems in computer science has endured because it keeps proving itself correct. The most defensible default is a time-to-live calibrated so that stale reads remain acceptable while the hit rate stays healthy. Event-driven invalidation — where a write emits a message that immediately evicts the relevant keys — offers greater precision, but it introduces an additional component that can fail quietly, leaving the cache indefinitely serving stale entries with no expiry to act as a safety net.
<!-- stay:EBbxHXpf hash=sha256:703d21f69911 -->

Regardless of which approach you adopt, track the hit rate in production. A cache that misses frequently is nothing but overhead: it imposes the cost of an extra lookup on every single read while almost never sparing the system from the slow path it was designed to avoid.
<!-- stay:scEyHzyH hash=sha256:cc8b69af499a -->

rewrites.before:
# How database indexes work
<!-- stay:b1eSVBcI hash=sha256:e8e45f27be40 -->

An index is a separate data structure that lets the database find rows without scanning
the whole table. Without one, a query that filters on a column must read every row and
test the predicate, which is linear in the size of the table. With an index on that
column, the database walks a sorted structure and jumps straight to the matching rows.
<!-- stay:X5oqxwzC hash=sha256:0d810dc9f72c -->

## B-trees
<!-- stay:TcWXqm8G hash=sha256:783124944320 -->

Most relational databases use a B-tree as the default index. A B-tree keeps keys sorted
and balanced so that lookups, range scans, and ordered traversal all run in logarithmic
time. Because the keys are sorted, a B-tree serves equality lookups, range queries, and
ORDER BY on the indexed column from the same structure.
<!-- stay:gR9JC0Cb hash=sha256:c7b7619c8d13 -->

The trade-off is write cost. Every insert, update, or delete that touches an indexed
column must also update the index, and a heavily indexed table pays that cost on every
write. A table optimized purely for write throughput keeps its index count low; a table
optimized for reads accepts more indexes to serve more query shapes.
<!-- stay:RW96GShV hash=sha256:3325de01273b -->

## Composite indexes and column order
<!-- stay:NIq6XcS4 hash=sha256:bd1998460f78 -->

An index on multiple columns is a composite index, and the order of the columns matters.
An index on (tenant_id, created_at) can serve a query that filters on tenant_id alone,
or on tenant_id and created_at together, because those are leftmost prefixes of the key.
It cannot efficiently serve a query that filters only on created_at, because created_at
is not a prefix. This leftmost-prefix rule is the single most common reason a query that
"has an index" still runs slowly.
<!-- stay:AG0OqD5K hash=sha256:706ecfbf653d -->

## Covering indexes
<!-- stay:3m2eCXd3 hash=sha256:ec95293bce52 -->

If an index contains every column a query reads, the database can answer the query from
the index alone and never touch the table. This is a covering index, and it can turn a
two-step lookup into one. The cost is a wider index that duplicates more data and slows
writes further, so covering indexes are worth it for hot queries and wasteful for rare
ones.
<!-- stay:zmhIEfDU hash=sha256:df35e954e8dd -->

## When not to index
<!-- stay:K1lbTHo0 hash=sha256:f0c2e56096e3 -->

Indexes are not free, and more is not better. An index on a low-cardinality column, such
as a boolean flag where almost every row has the same value, rarely helps because the
database still reads most of the table. The right indexes come from looking at the slow
queries the application actually runs, not from indexing every column in advance.
<!-- stay:kny6D1Dr hash=sha256:0bbf2350abcd -->

rewrites.after:
# How database indexes work
<!-- stay:b1eSVBcI hash=sha256:e8e45f27be40 -->

A database index is a dedicated data structure that allows rows to be located without reading the entire table. When no index exists, any query filtering on a column is forced to examine every row and evaluate the condition, a cost that grows linearly with table size. Once an index is built on that column, the database traverses a sorted structure and jumps directly to the relevant rows.
<!-- stay:X5oqxwzC hash=sha256:0d810dc9f72c -->

## B-trees
<!-- stay:TcWXqm8G hash=sha256:783124944320 -->

The default index type in most relational databases is the B-tree. Keys are maintained in sorted, balanced order so that lookups, range scans, and sequential traversal each complete in logarithmic time. A single B-tree structure is capable of handling equality searches, range queries, and ORDER BY operations on the indexed column.
<!-- stay:gR9JC0Cb hash=sha256:c7b7619c8d13 -->

The downside shows up on writes. Any insert, update, or delete that affects an indexed column requires the index to be updated as well, and a table carrying many indexes absorbs that penalty on every write operation. Tables designed around high write throughput should carry as few indexes as possible, while tables designed for read-heavy workloads can afford additional indexes to satisfy a wider variety of query patterns.
<!-- stay:RW96GShV hash=sha256:3325de01273b -->

## Composite indexes and column order
<!-- stay:NIq6XcS4 hash=sha256:bd1998460f78 -->

When an index spans multiple columns, it is called a composite index, and the sequence of those columns is critical. An index defined on (tenant_id, created_at) can efficiently support queries that filter on tenant_id by itself, or on both tenant_id and created_at together, since both represent leftmost prefixes of the key. A query filtering solely on created_at cannot use this index efficiently, because created_at does not appear at the front of the key. Misunderstanding this leftmost-prefix rule is by far the most frequent explanation for why a query remains slow despite an index being present.
<!-- stay:AG0OqD5K hash=sha256:706ecfbf653d -->

## Covering indexes
<!-- stay:3m2eCXd3 hash=sha256:ec95293bce52 -->

When every column a query needs is stored within the index itself, the database can satisfy the query entirely from the index without ever accessing the underlying table. An index that achieves this is called a covering index, and it collapses what would otherwise be a two-step retrieval into a single step. The drawback is that a wider index duplicates more data and imposes additional overhead on writes, making covering indexes a worthwhile investment for frequently executed queries but an unnecessary burden for infrequent ones.
<!-- stay:zmhIEfDU hash=sha256:df35e954e8dd -->

## When not to index
<!-- stay:K1lbTHo0 hash=sha256:f0c2e56096e3 -->

Indexes carry real costs, and adding more of them does not automatically improve performance. A column with very few distinct values, such as a boolean flag where the vast majority of rows share the same value, will rarely benefit from an index because the database ends up reading most of the table anyway. The right approach is to identify the slow queries the application genuinely executes and build indexes around those, rather than preemptively indexing every column.
<!-- stay:kny6D1Dr hash=sha256:0bbf2350abcd -->

rewrites.before:
# The three pillars of observability
<!-- stay:vQzzf99d hash=sha256:c80ecdf73c1b -->

Observability is the property of a system that lets you understand its internal state
from the outside. A service is observable when an operator can answer a new question
about its behaviour without shipping new code to expose the answer. The practice rests on
three kinds of telemetry: logs, metrics, and traces.
<!-- stay:ogdWsP51 hash=sha256:d45ac5db6bc4 -->

## Logs
<!-- stay:3uLGRZMJ hash=sha256:c8d9d134a246 -->

A log is a timestamped record of a discrete event. Logs are the oldest and most flexible
signal because a line can carry arbitrary context, but that flexibility is also their
weakness: unstructured text is hard to query at scale. Structured logging, where each
line is a set of key-value fields rather than a sentence, makes logs searchable and
aggregatable, and is the baseline expectation for any service running in production.
<!-- stay:KE4WJuGd hash=sha256:2d407f53c8ad -->

The discipline with logs is volume. A chatty service can generate gigabytes an hour, and
the cost of storing and indexing that quickly dwarfs the value. Log at the level the
question demands, sample high-volume events, and keep the noisy debug output behind a
flag you can turn on when you need it.
<!-- stay:3RZWyH5n hash=sha256:eb55c2ca6e16 -->

## Metrics
<!-- stay:b0AJJZZt hash=sha256:0452266c781f -->

A metric is a numeric measurement aggregated over time, such as request rate, error
rate, or latency. Metrics are cheap to store and fast to query because they are
pre-aggregated, which makes them the right tool for dashboards and alerts. The classic
starting set is the four golden signals: latency, traffic, errors, and saturation.
<!-- stay:O9W5cTAr hash=sha256:2a7914958255 -->

The limitation of a metric is that it summarizes. A latency metric tells you the
ninety-ninth percentile got worse; it cannot tell you which user or which code path
caused it. For that you need a signal that follows a single request.
<!-- stay:LImGwYmI hash=sha256:bddabc4a2066 -->

## Traces
<!-- stay:rsKnE54d hash=sha256:76b386f19db6 -->

A distributed trace follows one request across every service it touches, recording the
time spent in each. In a system of many small services, a trace is the only signal that
shows where a slow request actually spent its time, rather than which service merely
reported high latency. Each step is a span, and the spans together form a tree rooted at
the original request.
<!-- stay:qew04XhV hash=sha256:1acaac8266b7 -->

Used together, the three pillars answer different halves of the same question. Metrics
tell you something is wrong and alert you to it. Traces tell you where. Logs tell you
why. A team that invests in one and neglects the others ends up able to see the symptom
but never the cause.
<!-- stay:78j9laGT hash=sha256:696f7fa02dd6 -->

the cost of storing and indexing that quickly dwarfs the value. Log at the level the
question demands, sample high-volume events, and keep the noisy debug output behind a
flag you can turn on when you need it.
<!-- stay:3RZWyH5n hash=sha256:eb55c2ca6e16 -->

## Metrics
<!-- stay:b0AJJZZt hash=sha256:0452266c781f -->

A metric is a numeric measurement aggregated over time, such as request rate, error
rate, or latency. Metrics are cheap to store and fast to query because they are
pre-aggregated, which makes them the right tool for dashboards and alerts. The classic
starting set is the four golden signals: latency, traffic, errors, and saturation.
<!-- stay:O9W5cTAr hash=sha256:2a7914958255 -->

The limitation of a metric is that it summarizes. A latency metric tells you the
ninety-ninth percentile got worse; it cannot tell you which user or which code path
caused it. For that you need a signal that follows a single request.
<!-- stay:LImGwYmI hash=sha256:bddabc4a2066 -->

## Traces
<!-- stay:rsKnE54d hash=sha256:76b386f19db6 -->

A distributed trace follows one request across every service it touches, recording the
time spent in each. In a system of many small services, a trace is the only signal that
shows where a slow request actually spent its time, rather than which service merely
reported high latency. Each step is a span, and the spans together form a tree rooted at
the original request.
<!-- stay:qew04XhV hash=sha256:1acaac8266b7 -->

Used together, the three pillars answer different parts of the same question. Metrics
tell you something is wrong and alert you to it. Traces tell you where. Logs tell you
why. A team that invests in one and neglects the others ends up able to see the symptom
but never the cause.
<!-- stay:78j9laGT hash=sha256:696f7fa02dd6 -->

rewrites.before:
# The three pillars of observability
<!-- stay:vQzzf99d hash=sha256:c80ecdf73c1b -->

Observability is the property of a system that lets you understand its internal state
from the outside. A service is observable when an operator can answer a new question
about its behaviour without shipping new code to expose the answer. The practice rests on
three kinds of telemetry: logs, metrics, and traces.
<!-- stay:ogdWsP51 hash=sha256:d45ac5db6bc4 -->

## Logs
<!-- stay:3uLGRZMJ hash=sha256:c8d9d134a246 -->

A log is a timestamped record of a discrete event. Logs are the oldest and most flexible
signal because a line can carry arbitrary context, but that flexibility is also their
weakness: unstructured text is hard to query at scale. Structured logging, where each
line is a set of key-value fields rather than a sentence, makes logs searchable and
aggregatable, and is the baseline expectation for any service running in production.
<!-- stay:KE4WJuGd hash=sha256:2d407f53c8ad -->

The discipline with logs is volume. A chatty service can generate gigabytes an hour, and
the cost of storing and indexing that quickly dwarfs the value. Log at the level the
question demands, sample high-volume events, and keep the noisy debug output behind a
flag you can turn on when you need it.
<!-- stay:3RZWyH5n hash=sha256:eb55c2ca6e16 -->

## Metrics
<!-- stay:b0AJJZZt hash=sha256:0452266c781f -->

A metric is a numeric measurement aggregated over time, such as request rate, error
rate, or latency. Metrics are cheap to store and fast to query because they are
pre-aggregated, which makes them the right tool for dashboards and alerts. The classic
starting set is the four golden signals: latency, traffic, errors, and saturation.
<!-- stay:O9W5cTAr hash=sha256:2a7914958255 -->

The limitation of a metric is that it summarizes. A latency metric tells you the
ninety-ninth percentile got worse; it cannot tell you which user or which code path
caused it. For that you need a signal that follows a single request.
<!-- stay:LImGwYmI hash=sha256:bddabc4a2066 -->

## Traces
<!-- stay:rsKnE54d hash=sha256:76b386f19db6 -->

A distributed trace follows one request across every service it touches, recording the
time spent in each. In a system of many small services, a trace is the only signal that
shows where a slow request actually spent its time, rather than which service merely
reported high latency. Each step is a span, and the spans together form a tree rooted at
the original request.
<!-- stay:qew04XhV hash=sha256:1acaac8266b7 -->

Used together, the three pillars answer different halves of the same question. Metrics
tell you something is wrong and alert you to it. Traces tell you where. Logs tell you
why. A team that invests in one and neglects the others ends up able to see the symptom
but never the cause.
<!-- stay:78j9laGT hash=sha256:696f7fa02dd6 -->
rewrites.after:
# The three pillars of observability
<!-- stay:vQzzf99d hash=sha256:c80ecdf73c1b -->

Observability is a system's capacity to reveal its internal workings to an outside observer. When operators can answer unfamiliar questions about a service's behavior without deploying new code to surface that information, the service is considered observable. This capability is built on three categories of telemetry: logs, metrics, and traces.
<!-- stay:ogdWsP51 hash=sha256:d45ac5db6bc4 -->

## Logs
<!-- stay:3uLGRZMJ hash=sha256:c8d9d134a246 -->

A log is a discrete event captured alongside a timestamp. Because a single log line can hold any amount of contextual information, logs are both the oldest telemetry signal and the most adaptable — yet that same adaptability is their drawback: unstructured text becomes difficult to query as volume grows. The standard expectation for any production service is structured logging, where each entry consists of key-value fields rather than free-form prose, making the data both searchable and suitable for aggregation.
<!-- stay:KE4WJuGd hash=sha256:2d407f53c8ad -->

Volume is the central challenge with logs. A talkative service can pour out gigabytes every hour, and the expense of storing and indexing that data rapidly outweighs its usefulness. Match the log level to what the situation actually requires, apply sampling to high-frequency events, and keep verbose debug output hidden behind a flag that can be enabled on demand.
<!-- stay:3RZWyH5n hash=sha256:eb55c2ca6e16 -->

## Metrics
<!-- stay:b0AJJZZt hash=sha256:0452266c781f -->

A metric is a numerical value aggregated across time — think request rate, error rate, or latency. Because metrics are pre-aggregated, they are inexpensive to store and quick to query, which makes them the natural fit for dashboards and alerting. The canonical starting point is the four golden signals: latency, traffic, errors, and saturation.
<!-- stay:O9W5cTAr hash=sha256:2a7914958255 -->

The inherent limitation of a metric is that it condenses information. A latency metric can reveal that the ninety-ninth percentile has degraded, but it cannot identify which user or which code path is responsible. Answering that question requires a signal that tracks an individual request end to end.
<!-- stay:LImGwYmI hash=sha256:bddabc4a2066 -->

## Traces
<!-- stay:rsKnE54d hash=sha256:76b386f19db6 -->

A distributed trace accompanies a single request through every service it passes through, capturing how long each step takes. In an architecture composed of many small services, a trace is the only signal capable of pinpointing where a slow request actually lost time, as opposed to merely identifying which service reported elevated latency. Each individual step is called a span, and those spans are assembled into a tree whose root is the originating request.
<!-- stay:qew04XhV hash=sha256:1acaac8266b7 -->

When used in combination, the three pillars address complementary aspects of the same problem. Metrics detect that something has gone wrong and trigger an alert. Traces identify where in the system the problem lives. Logs explain the underlying reason. Any team that pours investment into one pillar while ignoring the others will find itself able to spot a symptom but perpetually unable to reach the cause.
<!-- stay:78j9laGT hash=sha256:696f7fa02dd6 -->

rewrites.before:
# Versioning a public API
<!-- stay:uUSVRylW hash=sha256:b290bb08b159 -->

Once an API has external consumers you no longer control, every change risks breaking
someone. Versioning is the contract that lets a service evolve without silently breaking
the clients that depend on it. The goal is not to avoid change, it is to make change
predictable.
<!-- stay:I01sCOwp hash=sha256:be1fa5abcc04 -->

## Backward-compatible changes
<!-- stay:QlXTvDMq hash=sha256:2fa9ed2f37ce -->

Many changes are safe and need no new version. Adding a new endpoint, adding an optional
field to a request, or adding a field to a response are all additive: an old client that
ignores the new field keeps working. The discipline is to treat unknown fields as
tolerable on both sides, so that adding one never breaks a careful consumer.
<!-- stay:tAHc2iI9 hash=sha256:31ddb1ce82ed -->

Breaking changes are different. Removing a field, renaming one, changing a type, or
making an optional field required all break clients that relied on the old shape. These
are the changes that require a version boundary.
<!-- stay:j3X6Ybiz hash=sha256:bdaceac4de04 -->

## URL versioning versus header versioning
<!-- stay:VK1mH5L3 hash=sha256:46f32e85bad0 -->

The most visible scheme puts the version in the URL path, such as /v1/orders and
/v2/orders. It is obvious, easy to route, and easy to test from a browser or a curl
command. The cost is that the version leaks into every link and every client, and a
major bump duplicates a lot of surface area.
<!-- stay:zvdSUVoC hash=sha256:762cd36c9be8 -->

Header versioning keeps the URL stable and selects the version through a request header
or a media type. It is cleaner in theory and keeps resource URLs canonical, but it is
invisible in a browser and easy for a client to forget, which is why many teams stay
with URL versioning despite its bluntness.
<!-- stay:5MAx9Zyd hash=sha256:7291bd75953f -->

## Deprecation
<!-- stay:BXJzMyNl hash=sha256:de9cfde469d6 -->

A version is only half the contract; the other half is how you retire one. A responsible
deprecation announces the date, returns a Deprecation header on the old version so
clients can detect it programmatically, and keeps the old version running long enough for
consumers to migrate. Cutting off a version without warning is the fastest way to lose
the trust that made the API worth integrating with.
<!-- stay:7gErHCru hash=sha256:93eeefb7cd4e -->

Keep a changelog that records every version and what changed in it. Consumers plan their
upgrades from that document, and a clear history is the difference between a smooth
migration and a flood of support tickets.
<!-- stay:PO0nm6Px hash=sha256:5647496f7018 -->

rewrites.after:
# Versioning a public API
<!-- stay:uUSVRylW hash=sha256:b290bb08b159 -->

When external consumers begin depending on an API you can no longer fully control, any modification carries the risk of breaking their integrations. A versioning strategy is the agreement that allows a service to grow and change without silently pulling the rug out from under its clients. The aim is not to freeze the API in place, but to ensure that change happens in a way everyone can anticipate.
<!-- stay:I01sCOwp hash=sha256:be1fa5abcc04 -->

## Backward-compatible changes
<!-- stay:QlXTvDMq hash=sha256:2fa9ed2f37ce -->

A large class of modifications can be made safely without introducing a new version at all. Introducing a fresh endpoint, appending an optional field to a request body, or including an extra field in a response are purely additive moves: an older client that does not recognize the new field continues to function without issue. The key discipline here is treating unrecognized fields as acceptable on both ends, so that adding one never catches a diligent consumer off guard.
<!-- stay:tAHc2iI9 hash=sha256:31ddb1ce82ed -->

Breaking changes operate by entirely different rules. Dropping a field, renaming it, altering its type, or promoting an optional field to required will all cause failures for clients built against the previous shape. It is precisely these modifications that demand a version boundary be drawn.
<!-- stay:j3X6Ybiz hash=sha256:bdaceac4de04 -->

## URL versioning versus header versioning
<!-- stay:VK1mH5L3 hash=sha256:46f32e85bad0 -->

The most straightforward approach embeds the version directly in the URL path — think /v1/orders alongside /v2/orders. This makes the version immediately apparent, simple to route at the infrastructure level, and trivial to exercise from a browser or a curl command. The tradeoff is that the version number propagates into every link and every client codebase, and a major increment ends up duplicating a substantial portion of the API surface.
<!-- stay:zvdSUVoC hash=sha256:762cd36c9be8 -->

Header versioning leaves the URL untouched and communicates the desired version through a request header or a negotiated media type. In principle this is a more elegant solution that preserves canonical resource URLs, but the version becomes invisible in a browser and is trivially easy for a client to omit — which is precisely why many teams continue to reach for URL versioning in spite of its coarser feel.
<!-- stay:5MAx9Zyd hash=sha256:7291bd75953f -->

## Deprecation
<!-- stay:BXJzMyNl hash=sha256:de9cfde469d6 -->

A version number covers only one side of the contract; the other side is the process by which you wind an old version down. Responsible deprecation means announcing a concrete end date, emitting a Deprecation header on responses from the old version so clients can detect the situation in code, and leaving the old version operational long enough that consumers have a realistic window to migrate. Shutting down a version without any notice is the surest way to destroy the trust that made your API worth building against in the first place.
<!-- stay:7gErHCru hash=sha256:93eeefb7cd4e -->

Maintain a changelog that documents every version and the specific changes each one introduced. Consumers rely on that record when planning their upgrade work, and a thorough, legible history is what separates an orderly migration from an avalanche of support requests.
<!-- stay:PO0nm6Px hash=sha256:5647496f7018 -->
